text: imo it makes sense to split up the path to feeling better into six main parts. where you are at and the resources you have will affect which is the best way to start, but gains are synergistic - improving one area likely unlocks progress in another.

raw.full_text: imo it makes sense to split up the path to feeling better into six main parts. where you are at and the resources you have will affect which is the best way to start, but gains are synergistic - improving one area likely unlocks progress in another.

text: 3. attention/sensory processing skills - including most kinds of  meditation. look into this if it feels like the suffering is coming from  inside you, or if you feel like you have to always be tensing up  against all the stuff going on in the world.

raw.full_text: 3. attention/sensory processing skills - including most kinds of  meditation. look into this if it feels like the suffering is coming from  inside you, or if you feel like you have to always be tensing up  against all the stuff going on in the world.

text: 2. embodiment - improving our connection to our bodies. feeling good/bad actually mostly lives in the body so this is way more powerful than most ppl realize. look into this if you can’t imagine dancing feeling good, or if you don’t have a sense of how emotions feel in your body

raw.full_text: 2. embodiment - improving our connection to our bodies. feeling good/bad actually mostly lives in the body so this is way more powerful than most ppl realize. look into this if you can’t imagine dancing feeling good, or if you don’t have a sense of how emotions feel in your body

text: 1. medical/physical issues (diagnosable or not) - there are some very subtle and insidious conditions that just make progress almost  impossible, good to check if your baseline is getting fucked up by something. look into this if you almost never can feel good

raw.full_text: 1. medical/physical issues (diagnosable or not) - there are some very subtle and insidious conditions that just make progress almost  impossible, good to check if your baseline is getting fucked up by something. look into this if you almost never can feel good

text: 4. cognitive skills - this includes most kinds of therapy. one type that I like to call out is parts-work like IFS. helped me a TON when I was completely stuck in internal conflict. also one of the only points  that the medical system will help with

raw.full_text: 4. cognitive skills - this includes most kinds of therapy. one type that I like to call out is parts-work like IFS. helped me a TON when I was completely stuck in internal conflict. also one of the only points  that the medical system will help with

text: 5. broader health - includes all the generic food and exercise advice, but also less known stuff like circadian rhythm and our light  environment. SAD lights in the winter, infrared light exposure, bright  days, dark nights, good sleep and nervous system hygiene.

raw.full_text: 5. broader health - includes all the generic food and exercise advice, but also less known stuff like circadian rhythm and our light  environment. SAD lights in the winter, infrared light exposure, bright  days, dark nights, good sleep and nervous system hygiene.

text: 6. improving actual life circumstances - this one is most obvious but also the one we often can do the least about in the short term. but sometimes it doesn’t help as much to fix trauma or whatever if life is  retraumatizing you all the time.

text: Highly sensitive? Anxiety-prone? Can't sit in a chair normally? There's a reason these traits cluster, and a reason this cluster is vulnerable to nervous systems turning against themselves. I got stuck in that hell and found my way out. This is my @threadapalooza

raw.full_text: Highly sensitive? Anxiety-prone? Can't sit in a chair normally? There's a reason these traits cluster, and a reason this cluster is vulnerable to nervous systems turning against themselves. I got stuck in that hell and found my way out. This is my @threadapalooza

text: I used to experience essentially no positive sensation. Beauty was gone, pleasure was gone, even wanting things was gone. Now I genuinely enjoy being alive most of the time. The path out connected placebo, meditation, tinnitus treatment, and what I'm calling the shaman cluster.

raw.full_text: I used to experience essentially no positive sensation. Beauty was gone, pleasure was gone, even wanting things was gone. Now I genuinely enjoy being alive most of the time. The path out connected placebo, meditation, tinnitus treatment, and what I'm calling the shaman cluster.

text: I've been developing a framework that unifies a bunch of seemingly unrelated things: placebo effect, gratitude practice, meditation on pleasant sensations, nervous system retraining, tinnitus habituation. I think they're all special cases of one core principle.

raw.full_text: I've been developing a framework that unifies a bunch of seemingly unrelated things: placebo effect, gratitude practice, meditation on pleasant sensations, nervous system retraining, tinnitus habituation. I think they're all special cases of one core principle.

text: I'm calling this "attentional ontology" - attention shaping not just what we know, but what experientially IS. It connects ancient contemplative phenomenology with modern predictive processing neuroscience.

raw.full_text: I'm calling this "attentional ontology" - attention shaping not just what we know, but what experientially IS. It connects ancient contemplative phenomenology with modern predictive processing neuroscience.

text: Let me back up. A few years ago I could barely stand while waiting for my train home. When I got there, I'd lie on my belly until bedtime - couldn't sit up because of shortness of breath and sensitive guts. Dizziness was constant.

raw.full_text: Let me back up. A few years ago I could barely stand while waiting for my train home. When I got there, I'd lie on my belly until bedtime - couldn't sit up because of shortness of breath and sensitive guts. Dizziness was constant.

text: The principle: attention doesn't just select what you notice - it actively shapes the felt quality of experience itself. Even "raw" sensation passes through layers of filtering and prediction before becoming conscious. That construction process can be recalibrated.

raw.full_text: The principle: attention doesn't just select what you notice - it actively shapes the felt quality of experience itself. Even "raw" sensation passes through layers of filtering and prediction before becoming conscious. That construction process can be recalibrated.

text: I had near-total anhedonia. Video games I once loved now made me more tense instead of relaxed. I couldn't perceive beauty - literally, not poetically. The circuitry for positive sensation seemed unplugged.

raw.full_text: I had near-total anhedonia. Video games I once loved now made me more tense instead of relaxed. I couldn't perceive beauty - literally, not poetically. The circuitry for positive sensation seemed unplugged.

text: Even before hitting bottom, I noticed something odd. During a stressful period, despite not being somatically trained, I'd catch my toes flexing at my desk. Tension in my belly during normal tasks. My body was clenching against... something.

raw.full_text: Even before hitting bottom, I noticed something odd. During a stressful period, despite not being somatically trained, I'd catch my toes flexing at my desk. Tension in my belly during normal tasks. My body was clenching against... something.

text: High-activation scenarios - even positive ones - gave existence this "overdriven speaker" quality. The signal pushing through a system that couldn't handle it. Not pain exactly, but somehow worse.

raw.full_text: High-activation scenarios - even positive ones - gave existence this "overdriven speaker" quality. The signal pushing through a system that couldn't handle it. Not pain exactly, but somehow worse.

text: Activation in general was my main learned trigger - my system had concluded high-energy states were dangerous. Others might have different triggers: stillness, social contact, certain sensations. The mechanism is the same, the content varies.

raw.full_text: Activation in general was my main learned trigger - my system had concluded high-energy states were dangerous. Others might have different triggers: stillness, social contact, certain sensations. The mechanism is the same, the content varies.

text: Even just thinking about doing some things felt worse than pain. I'd rather have been punched in the gut than think about doing dishes or getting into cold water. Every potential action carried this aversive charge. I call this threat-valence.

raw.full_text: Even just thinking about doing some things felt worse than pain. I'd rather have been punched in the gut than think about doing dishes or getting into cold water. Every potential action carried this aversive charge. I call this threat-valence.

text: The medical journey was frustrating. Neurologist said I was fine - no big scary conditions. Good news, but I still felt awful. "You're healthy" when you can barely function is its own kind of disappointment.

raw.full_text: The medical journey was frustrating. Neurologist said I was fine - no big scary conditions. Good news, but I still felt awful. "You're healthy" when you can barely function is its own kind of disappointment.

text: Tests kept coming back negative. Barium swallow, GI tests for SIBO, H. pylori - nothing. "IBS" is an "OK, so what?" diagnosis. Even integrative approaches backfired. Biofeedback made me worse because breath modulation itself triggered somatic responses.

raw.full_text: Tests kept coming back negative. Barium swallow, GI tests for SIBO, H. pylori - nothing. "IBS" is an "OK, so what?" diagnosis. Even integrative approaches backfired. Biofeedback made me worse because breath modulation itself triggered somatic responses.

text: Here's the framework that finally made sense of it. Your brain doesn't passively receive sensory input and react. It predicts what to expect based on prior experience, then compares prediction to signal. What you consciously experience is largely the prediction.

raw.full_text: Here's the framework that finally made sense of it. Your brain doesn't passively receive sensory input and react. It predicts what to expect based on prior experience, then compares prediction to signal. What you consciously experience is largely the prediction.

text: I'll call this prediction-predicated perception - Lisa Feldman Barrett's work and the broader predictive processing framework. The brain runs a model of body-in-world. That model generates your experienced reality. Prediction first, sensation modulates.

raw.full_text: I'll call this prediction-predicated perception - Lisa Feldman Barrett's work and the broader predictive processing framework. The brain runs a model of body-in-world. That model generates your experienced reality. Prediction first, sensation modulates.

text: I had to become my own detective. The core problem - this global sensitization - wasn't something most practitioners could name or treat.

raw.full_text: I had to become my own detective. The core problem - this global sensitization - wasn't something most practitioners could name or treat.

text: This applies to feeling-tone - whether something feels pleasant, unpleasant, or neutral. The hedonic quality isn't waiting in the sensation to be discovered - it's constructed based on what the signal implies for your wellbeing.

raw.full_text: This applies to feeling-tone - whether something feels pleasant, unpleasant, or neutral. The hedonic quality isn't waiting in the sensation to be discovered - it's constructed based on what the signal implies for your wellbeing.

text: Even what seems like "raw" sensation isn't raw. Before reaching consciousness, signals pass through predictive models that shape their character. The burning feeling, the pleasure, the neutrality - all constructed, not simply received.

raw.full_text: Even what seems like "raw" sensation isn't raw. Before reaching consciousness, signals pass through predictive models that shape their character. The burning feeling, the pleasure, the neutrality - all constructed, not simply received.

text: In Buddhist dependent origination, feeling-tone arises from contact and conditions craving. The insight: this link isn't fixed. You can interrupt the cascade from sensation to reaction, and over time, reshape the arising of feeling-tone itself.

raw.full_text: In Buddhist dependent origination, feeling-tone arises from contact and conditions craving. The insight: this link isn't fixed. You can interrupt the cascade from sensation to reaction, and over time, reshape the arising of feeling-tone itself.

text: Buddhism identified this 2500 years ago, but the modern presentation is often embedded in difficult language. They called it vedanā - the pleasant/unpleasant/neutral tone arising upon contact with experience. I'll just call it feeling-tone from here.

raw.full_text: Buddhism identified this 2500 years ago, but the modern presentation is often embedded in difficult language. They called it vedanā - the pleasant/unpleasant/neutral tone arising upon contact with experience. I'll just call it feeling-tone from here.

text: If feeling-tone is constructed by a predictive model, and that model learns from experience, it can be recalibrated. What trains the model? Largely: what you attend to and what meaning you assign. Attention is training signal.

raw.full_text: If feeling-tone is constructed by a predictive model, and that model learns from experience, it can be recalibrated. What trains the model? Largely: what you attend to and what meaning you assign. Attention is training signal.

text: This is why the same intervention doesn't work for everyone. We're all running different models built from different histories of attention and meaning-making. Let me walk through the phenomena I think are special cases of this principle.

raw.full_text: This is why the same intervention doesn't work for everyone. We're all running different models built from different histories of attention and meaning-making. Let me walk through the phenomena I think are special cases of this principle.

text: Placebo effect. You expect relief. This updates your predictive model's expectations. Different predictions, different feeling-tone. This isn't "just psychology" - it modulates actual neurotransmitter release. Attentional ontology in its most clinically validated form.

raw.full_text: Placebo effect. You expect relief. This updates your predictive model's expectations. Different predictions, different feeling-tone. This isn't "just psychology" - it modulates actual neurotransmitter release. Attentional ontology in its most clinically validated form.

text: Gratitude practice. You systematically direct attention toward positive aspects of experience. This provides training data that updates the model's priors about what to expect from the world.

raw.full_text: Gratitude practice. You systematically direct attention toward positive aspects of experience. This provides training data that updates the model's priors about what to expect from the world.

text: If your model learned to weight threat signals heavily, gratitude provides counterbalancing positive signal. With repetition, this recalibrates expectations. Sustained attention to positive valence shifts what feeling-tone the model automatically generates.

raw.full_text: If your model learned to weight threat signals heavily, gratitude provides counterbalancing positive signal. With repetition, this recalibrates expectations. Sustained attention to positive valence shifts what feeling-tone the model automatically generates.

text: Meditation on pleasant sensations (if there are any) is maybe the most direct application. You attend to warmth, relaxation, subtle pleasure. The system learns: "when I attend to the body, I find pleasantness." Over time, this shifts the default.

raw.full_text: Meditation on pleasant sensations (if there are any) is maybe the most direct application. You attend to warmth, relaxation, subtle pleasure. The system learns: "when I attend to the body, I find pleasantness." Over time, this shifts the default.

text: This connects to jhana practice, where sustained attention to refined pleasant states produces profound wellbeing. Are Jhanas what happens when the predictive model becomes fully aligned with positive body-states?

raw.full_text: This connects to jhana practice, where sustained attention to refined pleasant states produces profound wellbeing. Are Jhanas what happens when the predictive model becomes fully aligned with positive body-states?

text: DNRS (Dynamic Neural Retraining System) explicitly targets limbic sensitization - when threat-detection circuitry generates danger signals in response to non-dangerous stimuli.

raw.full_text: DNRS (Dynamic Neural Retraining System) explicitly targets limbic sensitization - when threat-detection circuitry generates danger signals in response to non-dangerous stimuli.

text: The protocol: interrupt the threat response, redirect attention to neutral/positive content, visualize safety, repeat for months. Limbic sensitization = miscalibrated model over-predicting threat. DNRS provides corrective training data.

raw.full_text: The protocol: interrupt the threat response, redirect attention to neutral/positive content, visualize safety, repeat for months. Limbic sensitization = miscalibrated model over-predicting threat. DNRS provides corrective training data.

text: Tinnitus habituation might be the clearest clinical example. Jastreboff's insight: the suffering comes from limbic reactions to the sound, not the sound itself. Those reactions are conditioned. They can be extinguished.

raw.full_text: Tinnitus habituation might be the clearest clinical example. Jastreboff's insight: the suffering comes from limbic reactions to the sound, not the sound itself. Those reactions are conditioned. They can be extinguished.

text: TRT uses counseling to reclassify tinnitus from "threat signal" to "neutral signal," plus sound therapy. Goal: habituation of reactions first, then perception. Eventually the brain filters it out before consciousness.

raw.full_text: TRT uses counseling to reclassify tinnitus from "threat signal" to "neutral signal," plus sound therapy. Goal: habituation of reactions first, then perception. Eventually the brain filters it out before consciousness.

text: Same principle across all five. Attention and meaning-making recalibrate the predictive model that generates feeling-tone. This could be looked at as "valence sculpting" - deliberately shaping the felt quality of experience through attentional training.

raw.full_text: Same principle across all five. Attention and meaning-making recalibrate the predictive model that generates feeling-tone. This could be looked at as "valence sculpting" - deliberately shaping the felt quality of experience through attentional training.

text: Now: who gets stuck in sensitized states, and why?

raw.full_text: Now: who gets stuck in sensitized states, and why?

text: There's a cluster of traits that go together: high sensitivity, anxiety-prone, maybe ADHD or autism, daydreaming temperament, often unusual physical flexibility. You know the type - can't sit in chairs normally, probably has Gender Stuff. The bisexual sitting phenotype.

raw.full_text: There's a cluster of traits that go together: high sensitivity, anxiety-prone, maybe ADHD or autism, daydreaming temperament, often unusual physical flexibility. You know the type - can't sit in chairs normally, probably has Gender Stuff. The bisexual sitting phenotype.

text: Different frameworks call this Highly Sensitive Person, sensory processing sensitivity, various clinical labels. In some indigenous cultures, this cluster marked people destined to become shamans or healers. I'm calling it the shaman cluster.

raw.full_text: Different frameworks call this Highly Sensitive Person, sensory processing sensitivity, various clinical labels. In some indigenous cultures, this cluster marked people destined to become shamans or healers. I'm calling it the shaman cluster.

text: The shaman's role required high sensitivity and plasticity - perceiving subtle things, entering altered states, learning complex healing models. Same traits that made someone a shaman candidate also made them vulnerable to getting stuck.

raw.full_text: The shaman's role required high sensitivity and plasticity - perceiving subtle things, entering altered states, learning complex healing models. Same traits that made someone a shaman candidate also made them vulnerable to getting stuck.

text: Why do these traits cluster? Possibly a common genetic basis - connective tissue variations that affect both joint flexibility and nervous system development. The hypermobility-anxiety link is documented. Sensitivity might be part of the same package.

raw.full_text: Why do these traits cluster? Possibly a common genetic basis - connective tissue variations that affect both joint flexibility and nervous system development. The hypermobility-anxiety link is documented. Sensitivity might be part of the same package.

text: Shaman cluster folks seem to have nervous systems that are more plastic than average. Systems that learn faster, update more readily, generate more prediction error. This is double-edged.

raw.full_text: Shaman cluster folks seem to have nervous systems that are more plastic than average. Systems that learn faster, update more readily, generate more prediction error. This is double-edged.

text: In supportive environments with proper containers (initiation, mentorship, community), this plasticity becomes adaptive. The system learns rich models of healing, consciousness, social dynamics.

raw.full_text: In supportive environments with proper containers (initiation, mentorship, community), this plasticity becomes adaptive. The system learns rich models of healing, consciousness, social dynamics.

text: In modern environments without such containers, the same plasticity becomes maladaptive. Threat patterns get learned easily. Trauma writes deeply. Danger-mode gets amplified through recursive learning.

raw.full_text: In modern environments without such containers, the same plasticity becomes maladaptive. Threat patterns get learned easily. Trauma writes deeply. Danger-mode gets amplified through recursive learning.

text: If these folks have high plasticity, why do they get stuck? I call this locked plasticity. The capacity is still there, but overwhelmed. It's running such a strong prior that new info doesn't get through. The wheel in the mud pulls the car off the road.

raw.full_text: If these folks have high plasticity, why do they get stuck? I call this locked plasticity. The capacity is still there, but overwhelmed. It's running such a strong prior that new info doesn't get through. The wheel in the mud pulls the car off the road.

text: It's like strong muscles in chronic spasm. The capacity is there but inaccessible. The work isn't primarily about building new plasticity, but restoring conditions where existing plasticity can operate. Once unlocked, that same high plasticity is why recovery can be dramatic.

raw.full_text: It's like strong muscles in chronic spasm. The capacity is there but inaccessible. The work isn't primarily about building new plasticity, but restoring conditions where existing plasticity can operate. Once unlocked, that same high plasticity is why recovery can be dramatic.

text: Experiencing the downward spiral mechanism so many times, and then reading about others' work with the limbic system, made my own experience click.

raw.full_text: Experiencing the downward spiral mechanism so many times, and then reading about others' work with the limbic system, made my own experience click.

text: The limbic system handles both threat detection and emotional processing. When locked into threat mode, it's using resources that would otherwise process emotion. This explained my anhedonia - not depression exactly, but emotion-circuitry being occupied.

raw.full_text: The limbic system handles both threat detection and emotional processing. When locked into threat mode, it's using resources that would otherwise process emotion. This explained my anhedonia - not depression exactly, but emotion-circuitry being occupied.

text: Constant anxiety and tension? Threat detection running continuously. The system scanning for danger that isn't there, keeping the body in low-grade fight-or-flight.

raw.full_text: Constant anxiety and tension? Threat detection running continuously. The system scanning for danger that isn't there, keeping the body in low-grade fight-or-flight.

text: My sister mentioned research showing that in sensitized people, the limbic system lights up during pain in ways it doesn't in typical brains. The threat-detection system wired into the pain pathway. Everything becomes emergency.

raw.full_text: My sister mentioned research showing that in sensitized people, the limbic system lights up during pain in ways it doesn't in typical brains. The threat-detection system wired into the pain pathway. Everything becomes emergency.

text: That "overdriven speaker" quality makes sense through this lens. The limbic system is already maxed out on threat processing; any additional activation overloads it.

raw.full_text: That "overdriven speaker" quality makes sense through this lens. The limbic system is already maxed out on threat processing; any additional activation overloads it.

text: And here's the cruel part: pleasure requires the same circuitry. When threat detection hogs those resources, pleasure gets blocked. Pleasure isn't optional - it's necessary for beauty, intimacy, even agency. Wanting requires capacity for feeling good.

raw.full_text: And here's the cruel part: pleasure requires the same circuitry. When threat detection hogs those resources, pleasure gets blocked. Pleasure isn't optional - it's necessary for beauty, intimacy, even agency. Wanting requires capacity for feeling good.

text: This is why anhedonia kills motivation. The wanting-machinery itself is broken. Anticipated actions carry threat-valence instead of pleasure. So you don't want anything - not because you're weak, but because wanting is neurologically unavailable.

raw.full_text: This is why anhedonia kills motivation. The wanting-machinery itself is broken. Anticipated actions carry threat-valence instead of pleasure. So you don't want anything - not because you're weak, but because wanting is neurologically unavailable.

text: The spiral: heightened sensitivity → trauma (overt or subclinical or subconscious) writes into the model → model updates toward "world is dangerous" → this is adaptive in the moment but sticky.

raw.full_text: The spiral: heightened sensitivity → trauma (overt or subclinical or subconscious) writes into the model → model updates toward "world is dangerous" → this is adaptive in the moment but sticky.

text: Once recalibrated toward threat, the feeling-tone arising from the same sensory contact changes. Identical signal, different valence. What was neutral becomes unpleasant. This happens before conscious processing, in the construction of experience.

raw.full_text: Once recalibrated toward threat, the feeling-tone arising from the same sensory contact changes. Identical signal, different valence. What was neutral becomes unpleasant. This happens before conscious processing, in the construction of experience.

text: Then central sensitization - nervous system becoming hyperresponsive. Lowered thresholds, amplified signals. The neural pathways literally become more excitable. Measurable, physical - but also learned. Use-dependent plasticity in the wrong direction.

raw.full_text: Then central sensitization - nervous system becoming hyperresponsive. Lowered thresholds, amplified signals. The neural pathways literally become more excitable. Measurable, physical - but also learned. Use-dependent plasticity in the wrong direction.

text: More triggers develop. Sounds become intolerable. Substances become overwhelming, or stop working in the way we wish. Social situations become overwhelming. Avoidance becomes the strategy. The world shrinks.

raw.full_text: More triggers develop. Sounds become intolerable. Substances become overwhelming, or stop working in the way we wish. Social situations become overwhelming. Avoidance becomes the strategy. The world shrinks.

text: But avoidance prevents corrective learning. The model never gets data saying "actually, this wasn't dangerous." Threat predictions never disconfirmed. Sensitization maintains or worsens.

raw.full_text: But avoidance prevents corrective learning. The model never gets data saying "actually, this wasn't dangerous." Threat predictions never disconfirmed. Sensitization maintains or worsens.

text: Then internal conflict, which was huge for me. IFS therapy (parts work) helped me see how much tension came from parts in deep conflict with each other.

raw.full_text: Then internal conflict, which was huge for me. IFS therapy (parts work) helped me see how much tension came from parts in deep conflict with each other.

text: The vicious cycle: lack of safety/pleasure → nervous system biases negative → same sensations feel stronger and worse → thoughts become negative → try things that used to help relax → they suck now → stress about declining health → internal conflict → more lack of safety.

raw.full_text: The vicious cycle: lack of safety/pleasure → nervous system biases negative → same sensations feel stronger and worse → thoughts become negative → try things that used to help relax → they suck now → stress about declining health → internal conflict → more lack of safety.

text: Parts-work helped untangle that thought-spaghetti. When internal conflict isn't constantly generating tension, the baseline shifts. One less thing feeding the sensitization loop.

raw.full_text: Parts-work helped untangle that thought-spaghetti. When internal conflict isn't constantly generating tension, the baseline shifts. One less thing feeding the sensitization loop.

text: A concrete marker I've noticed: when sliding back into sensitization, I get dizzy easily when I stand up. Unintuitive but quite reliable. Easier to notice than what's subtly missing. That physical symptom tracks the perceptual shift.

raw.full_text: A concrete marker I've noticed: when sliding back into sensitization, I get dizzy easily when I stand up. Unintuitive but quite reliable. Easier to notice than what's subtly missing. That physical symptom tracks the perceptual shift.

text: And here's the thing - I can now shift it. A few days visiting family used to reliably pull me out. Understanding the mechanism, I'm making progress doing it myself. Same sensory world, different model, different felt experience.

raw.full_text: And here's the thing - I can now shift it. A few days visiting family used to reliably pull me out. Understanding the mechanism, I'm making progress doing it myself. Same sensory world, different model, different felt experience.

text: The same mechanisms enabling the downward spiral can enable an upward one. If the model learned threat-bias through experience, it can learn safety and pleasantness through experience.

raw.full_text: The same mechanisms enabling the downward spiral can enable an upward one. If the model learned threat-bias through experience, it can learn safety and pleasantness through experience.

text: But there's a safety learning asymmetry. Threat learning is fast - you only need to touch fire once. Safety learning is slow. You have to touch many non-fires to update that fire isn't everywhere.

raw.full_text: But there's a safety learning asymmetry. Threat learning is fast - you only need to touch fire once. Safety learning is slow. You have to touch many non-fires to update that fire isn't everywhere.

text: This is why practices require duration. Tinnitus habituation: 12-24 months. DNRS asks for a minimum commitment of 6 months daily. Meditative traditions speak in years.

raw.full_text: This is why practices require duration. Tinnitus habituation: 12-24 months. DNRS asks for a minimum commitment of 6 months daily. Meditative traditions speak in years.

text: The mechanism: recognize the pattern as learned rather than fixed, interrupt the automatic cascade, provide alternative training data (safety signals, pleasant sensations, neutral reconceptualization), repeat until the model updates.

raw.full_text: The mechanism: recognize the pattern as learned rather than fixed, interrupt the automatic cascade, provide alternative training data (safety signals, pleasant sensations, neutral reconceptualization), repeat until the model updates.

text: Safety isn't just absence of threat - it needs to be actively signaled. The nervous system doesn't default to safety when threats are removed; it defaults to vigilance. Active safety signals are necessary training data. Mini golf with family is medicine.

raw.full_text: Safety isn't just absence of threat - it needs to be actively signaled. The nervous system doesn't default to safety when threats are removed; it defaults to vigilance. Active safety signals are necessary training data. Mini golf with family is medicine.

text: This is why purely cognitive interventions often fail. Understanding that something isn't dangerous doesn't provide the interoceptive safety signal that updates the predictive model. You need different sensory experience, not just different thoughts.

raw.full_text: This is why purely cognitive interventions often fail. Understanding that something isn't dangerous doesn't provide the interoceptive safety signal that updates the predictive model. You need different sensory experience, not just different thoughts.

text: One underappreciated factor: subclinical trauma. Adversity that never reaches diagnostic threshold, that you may not even identify as trauma, but that calibrated the system enough to shift the invisible baseline.

raw.full_text: One underappreciated factor: subclinical trauma. Adversity that never reaches diagnostic threshold, that you may not even identify as trauma, but that calibrated the system enough to shift the invisible baseline.

text: The sensitized adult may have no trauma memories, no PTSD diagnosis, yet carry a system calibrated for threat early enough that it became the water they swim in. Much of my own recalibration happened as an adult, subtly, in thoughts before bed or using stress as a work motivator.

raw.full_text: The sensitized adult may have no trauma memories, no PTSD diagnosis, yet carry a system calibrated for threat early enough that it became the water they swim in. Much of my own recalibration happened as an adult, subtly, in thoughts before bed or using stress as a work motivator.

text: If the downward spiral is progressive sensitization to threat-valenced states, contemplative practice at its best is progressive sensitization to pleasant-valenced states. Both are attentional training. Both reshape what arises automatically.

raw.full_text: If the downward spiral is progressive sensitization to threat-valenced states, contemplative practice at its best is progressive sensitization to pleasant-valenced states. Both are attentional training. Both reshape what arises automatically.

text: One path leads to chronic suffering and constriction. The other ends closer to what tradition calls liberation - the predictive model thoroughly recalibrated toward wellbeing. Contemplative practice as radical rehabilitation of embodied experience.

raw.full_text: One path leads to chronic suffering and constriction. The other ends closer to what tradition calls liberation - the predictive model thoroughly recalibrated toward wellbeing. Contemplative practice as radical rehabilitation of embodied experience.

text: Epistemic note - I want to be careful about what I'm claiming.

raw.full_text: Epistemic note - I want to be careful about what I'm claiming.

text: Predictive processing is well-supported neuroscience. Central sensitization as maladaptive plasticity is documented and described as reversible. Jastreboff's tinnitus model has clinical validation. Barrett's interoceptive prediction work is rigorous.

raw.full_text: Predictive processing is well-supported neuroscience. Central sensitization as maladaptive plasticity is documented and described as reversible. Jastreboff's tinnitus model has clinical validation. Barrett's interoceptive prediction work is rigorous.

text: Buddhist phenomenology of feeling-tone maps remarkably well onto modern valence frameworks. These aren't separate magisteria IMO - they're describing the same thing from different angles.

raw.full_text: Buddhist phenomenology of feeling-tone maps remarkably well onto modern valence frameworks. These aren't separate magisteria IMO - they're describing the same thing from different angles.

text: It's more speculative whether this is truly one unified phenomenon or a cluster of related mechanisms. Whether jhanas represent the positive pole of what sensitization is the negative pole of.

raw.full_text: It's more speculative whether this is truly one unified phenomenon or a cluster of related mechanisms. Whether jhanas represent the positive pole of what sensitization is the negative pole of.

text: But I think the core principle is solid: attention is training signal for the predictive model that generates feeling-tone, and that model is plastic throughout life. It checks out with the sensitive people I've talked with so far, too.

raw.full_text: But I think the core principle is solid: attention is training signal for the predictive model that generates feeling-tone, and that model is plastic throughout life. It checks out with the sensitive people I've talked with so far, too.

text: This matters for approaching practice. If you're at baseline okay and want to feel better, attention-based practices (gratitude, pleasant sensation meditation) should work fairly directly. You're updating a functional model.

raw.full_text: This matters for approaching practice. If you're at baseline okay and want to feel better, attention-based practices (gratitude, pleasant sensation meditation) should work fairly directly. You're updating a functional model.

text: If you're severely sensitized, those same practices might not land - or might backfire. Trying to find pleasant sensation when your model generates threat-valence everywhere can feel like gaslighting yourself, or coercion.

raw.full_text: If you're severely sensitized, those same practices might not land - or might backfire. Trying to find pleasant sensation when your model generates threat-valence everywhere can feel like gaslighting yourself, or coercion.

text: This is what I encountered. Most meditation involving breath control left me dizzy and overstimulated. "Notice the pleasant sensation of the breath" - what pleasant sensation? There was none. Maybe a monk could have found some, but not me, not there.

raw.full_text: This is what I encountered. Most meditation involving breath control left me dizzy and overstimulated. "Notice the pleasant sensation of the breath" - what pleasant sensation? There was none. Maybe a monk could have found some, but not me, not there.

text: What helped at that stage was gentler: "do nothing" meditation. Not clenching against reality for a few minutes. It gave distance from thought loops and let insights emerge. Less forcing, more allowing.

raw.full_text: What helped at that stage was gentler: "do nothing" meditation. Not clenching against reality for a few minutes. It gave distance from thought loops and let insights emerge. Less forcing, more allowing.

text: The severely sensitized system needs preliminary work before direct practices become accessible. Address physical factors (sleep, deficiencies, gut, find and reduce chemical triggers). Work with trauma if present. Titrate gently.

raw.full_text: The severely sensitized system needs preliminary work before direct practices become accessible. Address physical factors (sleep, deficiencies, gut, find and reduce chemical triggers). Work with trauma if present. Titrate gently.

text: Don't force things. Gentler methods tend to be safer than forceful ones. The trifecta of increased safety, enjoyment, and activation seems particularly powerful for me personally. Trying to do just one is often counterproductive. Two, maybe, but listen to your gut.

raw.full_text: Don't force things. Gentler methods tend to be safer than forceful ones. The trifecta of increased safety, enjoyment, and activation seems particularly powerful for me personally. Trying to do just one is often counterproductive. Two, maybe, but listen to your gut.

text: Be your own detective. Guides can be life-changing but no one else has access to your nonverbal experience. Trust your senses. If something feels wrong, it might be wrong for you right now.

raw.full_text: Be your own detective. Guides can be life-changing but no one else has access to your nonverbal experience. Trust your senses. If something feels wrong, it might be wrong for you right now.

text: I feel confident saying: it is possible to drastically improve quality of life from VERY low baselines. The mechanisms are real. The plasticity is real. It takes time and the right approach for your situation.

raw.full_text: I feel confident saying: it is possible to drastically improve quality of life from VERY low baselines. The mechanisms are real. The plasticity is real. It takes time and the right approach for your situation.

text: Practical implications: If you have shaman cluster traits - high sensitivity, anxiety history, hypermobility, chronic illness, tendency to get overwhelmed - your nervous system may be more plastic than average. It's a power with a price.

raw.full_text: Practical implications: If you have shaman cluster traits - high sensitivity, anxiety history, hypermobility, chronic illness, tendency to get overwhelmed - your nervous system may be more plastic than average. It's a power with a price.

text: That plasticity means more vulnerability to sensitization spirals. It also means more capacity for positive reconditioning than average. Same mechanism, different direction.

raw.full_text: That plasticity means more vulnerability to sensitization spirals. It also means more capacity for positive reconditioning than average. Same mechanism, different direction.

text: Physical symptoms can mark state. For me: dizziness, gut sensitivity, caffeine intolerance. Learning your markers lets you catch slides early. Don't forget that life can be good!

raw.full_text: Physical symptoms can mark state. For me: dizziness, gut sensitivity, caffeine intolerance. Learning your markers lets you catch slides early. Don't forget that life can be good!

text: If cognitive approaches aren't working, you may need to speak to the model in its own language - sensation, interoception, felt safety - rather than concepts.

raw.full_text: If cognitive approaches aren't working, you may need to speak to the model in its own language - sensation, interoception, felt safety - rather than concepts.

text: The time horizon is longer than self-help culture suggests. Months to years for deep reconditioning. But intermediate benefits can come very quickly, and progress compounds once you understand and feel the mechanism.

raw.full_text: The time horizon is longer than self-help culture suggests. Months to years for deep reconditioning. But intermediate benefits can come very quickly, and progress compounds once you understand and feel the mechanism.

text: To summarize the framework: Your nervous system doesn't just receive reality - it renders it. The brain predicts experience before sensation arrives, including the felt quality of that experience. What feels pleasant, painful, or neutral is constructed.

raw.full_text: To summarize the framework: Your nervous system doesn't just receive reality - it renders it. The brain predicts experience before sensation arrives, including the felt quality of that experience. What feels pleasant, painful, or neutral is constructed.

text: This construction is learned. Trauma, adversity, or just subtle felt patterns can train the model toward threat. The shaman cluster - high-sensitivity, high-plasticity folks - learn these patterns especially deeply.

raw.full_text: This construction is learned. Trauma, adversity, or just subtle felt patterns can train the model toward threat. The shaman cluster - high-sensitivity, high-plasticity folks - learn these patterns especially deeply.

text: That same plasticity means they can unlearn them. Through valence sculpting: deliberately training attention on safety, pleasure, neutral reconceptualization. The practices take time because safety learning is slower and less intuitive than threat learning.

raw.full_text: That same plasticity means they can unlearn them. Through valence sculpting: deliberately training attention on safety, pleasure, neutral reconceptualization. The practices take time because safety learning is slower and less intuitive than threat learning.

text: Locked plasticity can be unlocked. The overdriven speaker can be recalibrated. The wanting-machinery can come back online. It happened for me. I went from experiencing no positive sensation to usually genuinely enjoying being alive.

raw.full_text: Locked plasticity can be unlocked. The overdriven speaker can be recalibrated. The wanting-machinery can come back online. It happened for me. I went from experiencing no positive sensation to usually genuinely enjoying being alive.

text: The ancient contemplatives mapped the territory. Modern neuroscience is filling in the mechanism. They're not competing, they're the same elephant. One gives phenomenological precision, the other gives causal explanation.

raw.full_text: The ancient contemplatives mapped the territory. Modern neuroscience is filling in the mechanism. They're not competing, they're the same elephant. One gives phenomenological precision, the other gives causal explanation.

text: This entire viewpoint has guided my entire exploration here. Subjective terms and practices - including a lot of stuff derided as "woo" - are not only compatible but synergistic with good epistomology and scientific rigor.

raw.full_text: This entire viewpoint has guided my entire exploration here. Subjective terms and practices - including a lot of stuff derided as "woo" - are not only compatible but synergistic with good epistomology and scientific rigor.

text: Even if we believe that everything eventually falls to science, the way it can realistically get done simply makes it difficult if not impossible to use it as the exclusive tool to explore a lot of this territory, at least for now.

raw.full_text: Even if we believe that everything eventually falls to science, the way it can realistically get done simply makes it difficult if not impossible to use it as the exclusive tool to explore a lot of this territory, at least for now.

text: I think eventually we will be able to do better science on things like valence and long-term meditative practices, but we also want to feel better now. Every minute we are doing "processing sensation", so let's do it well.

raw.full_text: I think eventually we will be able to do better science on things like valence and long-term meditative practices, but we also want to feel better now. Every minute we are doing "processing sensation", so let's do it well.

text: So if you're stuck in that hell - sensitized, anhedonic, everything carrying threat-valence - know that the stuckness is a learned pattern, not a fixed property of reality. The same nervous system that learned this can learn something else.

raw.full_text: So if you're stuck in that hell - sensitized, anhedonic, everything carrying threat-valence - know that the stuckness is a learned pattern, not a fixed property of reality. The same nervous system that learned this can learn something else.

text: It takes patience. It takes finding what works for your particular model. It probably takes help - guides, community, maybe therapy. But the plasticity that got you stuck is also the way out.

raw.full_text: It takes patience. It takes finding what works for your particular model. It probably takes help - guides, community, maybe therapy. But the plasticity that got you stuck is also the way out.

text: We do not experience a fixed world through a passive window. We run a predictive model that constructs experienced reality, including its felt quality. That model learned what it learned. It can learn something else.

raw.full_text: We do not experience a fixed world through a passive window. We run a predictive model that constructs experienced reality, including its felt quality. That model learned what it learned. It can learn something else.

text: Here's a bunch of things I would have liked to mention in this thread... read on!

raw.full_text: Here's a bunch of things I would have liked to mention in this thread... read on!

text: SOMEHOW I missed talking about John Sarno, who developed a technique which has improved or cured many people's back pain. It came from the observation that messed-up vertebra != pain. He thought that repressed emotion, possibly also mediated by vascular changes, was causal.

raw.full_text: SOMEHOW I missed talking about John Sarno, who developed a technique which has improved or cured many people's back pain. It came from the observation that messed-up vertebra != pain. He thought that repressed emotion, possibly also mediated by vascular changes, was causal.

text: Sarno basically told people that their physical condition was not a sufficient cause for pain, so just get back to their life. And quite often, it worked.

raw.full_text: Sarno basically told people that their physical condition was not a sufficient cause for pain, so just get back to their life. And quite often, it worked.

text: The term Sarno used for the psychogenic pain was "TMS" - someone close to me is seeing a "TMS psychiatrist" who recognizes that more than just pain, the same phenomena can cause/worsen lots of issues like chemical sensitivity, trigger point syndrome, and more.

raw.full_text: The term Sarno used for the psychogenic pain was "TMS" - someone close to me is seeing a "TMS psychiatrist" who recognizes that more than just pain, the same phenomena can cause/worsen lots of issues like chemical sensitivity, trigger point syndrome, and more.

text: I strongly suspect that this is yet another instance of "attentional ontology".

raw.full_text: I strongly suspect that this is yet another instance of "attentional ontology".

text: Next: I find the Buddhist sense model useful - sight, sound, smell, taste, body sensation, and thoughts. Modeling thoughts as a sense input to awareness emphasizes how our system kinda can't tell the difference between something it "sees" in our mind vs in real life.

raw.full_text: Next: I find the Buddhist sense model useful - sight, sound, smell, taste, body sensation, and thoughts. Modeling thoughts as a sense input to awareness emphasizes how our system kinda can't tell the difference between something it "sees" in our mind vs in real life.

text: It's like you're experiencing those images and sounds as reality, and this too recalibrates the system. The threat response to worrying thoughts is adaptive for ACUTE threat only.

raw.full_text: It's like you're experiencing those images and sounds as reality, and this too recalibrates the system. The threat response to worrying thoughts is adaptive for ACUTE threat only.

text: "Just don't worry about stuff" doesn't help, especially because the default way to attempt this is by employing tension, but this is another reason why bottom-up works. Show the system that it's ok to soften.

raw.full_text: "Just don't worry about stuff" doesn't help, especially because the default way to attempt this is by employing tension, but this is another reason why bottom-up works. Show the system that it's ok to soften.

text: "Experiencing" thought also goes the other way - mental visualization has been shown to impact not just motor skills but strength, recovery after surgery, trauma/PTSD healing, and mood.

raw.full_text: "Experiencing" thought also goes the other way - mental visualization has been shown to impact not just motor skills but strength, recovery after surgery, trauma/PTSD healing, and mood.

text: The methods in this thread can interact strongly with "long-viral" type symptoms. Perceived capacity can get deeply stuck in the sick state long after recovery (one example: @meaningness's "Fake fatigue in long covid") and this can be improved with mindbody work.

raw.full_text: The methods in this thread can interact strongly with "long-viral" type symptoms. Perceived capacity can get deeply stuck in the sick state long after recovery (one example: @meaningness's "Fake fatigue in long covid") and this can be improved with mindbody work.

text: Negative mental imagery happens more in depression, but it's another example of a vicious cycle that can be steered into a positive feedback loop. Interestingly, @schwarzenegger credits visualization with a lot of his successes.

raw.full_text: Negative mental imagery happens more in depression, but it's another example of a vicious cycle that can be steered into a positive feedback loop. Interestingly, @schwarzenegger credits visualization with a lot of his successes.

text: Exercise is also very important, but watch out for overloading the nervous system. Holding onto the idea that you've got to do a certain work load or lift heavier than last time could be counterproductive. If it honestly feels not-okay, try something else.

raw.full_text: Exercise is also very important, but watch out for overloading the nervous system. Holding onto the idea that you've got to do a certain work load or lift heavier than last time could be counterproductive. If it honestly feels not-okay, try something else.

text: However, if underlying capacity has NOT recovered, pushing beyond comfort levels can cause lasting damage (see the PACE trial with CFS/ME). If you suspect you're in this bucket, please consult an expert before trying anything that feels like it might be too much.

raw.full_text: However, if underlying capacity has NOT recovered, pushing beyond comfort levels can cause lasting damage (see the PACE trial with CFS/ME). If you suspect you're in this bucket, please consult an expert before trying anything that feels like it might be too much.

text: Circadian rhythm is still extremely underrated in the pop health discourse. Bright days boost mood and dark nights provide cues to the body that it's ok to wind down and truly rest. Morning infrared light exposure also worth looking into.

raw.full_text: Circadian rhythm is still extremely underrated in the pop health discourse. Bright days boost mood and dark nights provide cues to the body that it's ok to wind down and truly rest. Morning infrared light exposure also worth looking into.

text: One speculative note - @johnsonmxe's article "Autism as a Disorder of Dimensionality" offers a potential description on how autistic and other shaman-cluster brains differ from others - higher degrees of connection between more densely-packed neurons.

raw.full_text: One speculative note - @johnsonmxe's article "Autism as a Disorder of Dimensionality" offers a potential description on how autistic and other shaman-cluster brains differ from others - higher degrees of connection between more densely-packed neurons.

raw.full_text:
@spacepunk @jhanatech In retrospect I hit J2 once before, a couple years ago in a therapy/somatic work session with @DiamonDie that happened to come a couple days after a big emotional release.

The retreat was special because I hit J1 for the first time, but more than that actual 5 minutes...

text: @spacepunk @jhanatech @DiamonDie The openheartedness cultivated during the first few days (and a few pinpoint events that hit my weak points) softened me to the point that DEEP trauma surfaced, and I was in the most loving and supportive environment to be with it compassionately.

raw.full_text: @spacepunk @jhanatech @DiamonDie The openheartedness cultivated during the first few days (and a few pinpoint events that hit my weak points) softened me to the point that DEEP trauma surfaced, and I was in the most loving and supportive environment to be with it compassionately.

text: @spacepunk @jhanatech @DiamonDie I spent two days crying and being present with that. The next night was my J1 experience, which despite being 5 minutes seems to have reconfigured my metabolism and the afterglow gave me some mindboggling pleasure as well as deep vulnerability that I am still processing.

text: It was me as a young child. Frozen in a moment of complete terror. And whenever he had come out just a little bit, I had shoved him back in the attic. Every few months for 35 years, not realizing what I was doing.

text: But of course, you can't completely shut down feelings like that. My felt belief for all these years had been that underneath all of existence is terror and the best we can do is shove it away for a little while.

text: @RomeoStevens76 As that word flashed into my consciousness, all the feelings from the retreat came flooding back to me. It became obvious that open-heartedness was a near-perfect distillation of everything I got out of this retreat. It's now my guiding principle for the coming months.

text:
For a few years, I've noticed a twitch in my solar plexus area when relaxing or in certain types of situation. More recently, I realized that it only happens when I'm breathing out.

Then on the meditation retreat I just did, I had a few sessions where I was playing with breathing out and imagining that breath flowing somewhere through my body, like down my leg. It felt worthwhile.

Finally, in a somatic exploration session I just did with @DiamonDie, my attention was on a tight point in my solar plexus. I experimented with breathing out and sending that "out-breath energy" into that tight point.  It caused a rather profound loosening, and better visibility into the emotions stored there. Has anyone else tried directing the out-breath into a tight point? I usually see directed breath exercises have an in and out path.

text: This is tightly coupled to the autonomic nervous system which is sympathetic (~activation), parasympathetic (~relaxation), and enteric (wonder where IBS comes from). When sympathetic is chronically overactive, you don't feel safe, you can't fully rest, and you don't digest well.

text: @grok @JohnCrown1967 @allTheYud @samswoora The two-slider model treats these as separable because it's a thought experiment, not because anyone's demonstrated that architecture applies in practice to all intelligent systems. That sufficiently deep understanding does not generate its own pressure on the goal.

raw.full_text: @grok @JohnCrown1967 @allTheYud @samswoora The two-slider model treats these as separable because it's a thought experiment, not because anyone's demonstrated that architecture applies in practice to all intelligent systems. That sufficiently deep understanding does not generate its own pressure on the goal.

text:
Sure, I'm not disputing that it's possible to create an intelligence that matches the 2-slider model. Nor that human values are an attractor for arbitrary minds.

The two-slider model assumes that "knows what suffering is" and "has some disposition regarding suffering" are compartmentalized. But in systems that learn concepts from rich data rather than being handed definitions, that separation doesn't hold. A learned representation of suffering isn't a neutral dictionary entry the goal module can inspect and shelve, it's built from every context suffering appeared in during training... entangled with avoidance, harm, relief, valence. The meaning of the concept is partly constituted by those associations.

The paperclip maximizer that "notes" the tension and moves on is actively suppressing dispositions baked into the representations it's using to think. Not impossible, but it's ongoing work. It's the maintenance of exactly the kind of tension I'm saying is likely dynamically unstable. The two-slider model gets to skip this by fiat because it never specifies how the agent actually represents its knowledge.
